content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

December 30, 1997


Jean Cihigoyenetche
Cihigoyenetche, Grossberg & Clouse
3602 Inland Empire Blvd., Suite C-315
Ontario, California 91764

Re: Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-97-574

Dear Mr. Cihigoyenetche:

     This letter is a response to your request for advice under the Political Reform Act ("the Act") on behalf of Terry Catlin regarding potential conflicts of interest arising out of his service as a boardmember for the Chino Basin Municipal Water District.

     Please bear in mind that nothing in this letter should be construed as evaluation of any conduct which may already have taken place. Further, this letter is based on the facts you have presented to us. The Commission does not act as finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTIONS

1. What is the appropriate regulatory standard for determining whether the effect of a governmental decision would be "material" to Sunkist Growers, Inc., a non-stock membership cooperative of citrus growers?

2. Are the "public generally" provisions of Regulation 18703 applicable, when District decisions on rates and charges affect all users of certain District assets?


¹ Government Code sections 81000—91015. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18000—18995 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 450, Sacramento, California 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. A-97-574
Page 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. Materiality of effect on Sunkist should be measured against the standards given at
subdivisions (e), (f), or (g) of Regulation 18702.2. The value of Sunkist's net tangible assets
determines which of these subdivisions will be applicable.

2. We do not have sufficient information to decide all the possibilities you raise with this
question, but on the facts as known to us, it does not appear that the "public generally" exception
can be applied to a decision affecting users of certain District assets.

FACTS

The Chino Basin Municipal Water District (the "District") operates a non-reclaimable
waste line ("NRW") which transfers industrial waste for offsite treatment. In exchange for the
use of the NRW line, the District charges industrial users an initial buy-in fee for the purchase of
capacity rights, and also charges user fees based upon the volume of waste the particular industry
discharges into the system.

Mr. Catlin is employed as a chemist with the Sunkist Corporation, and has been so
employed for many years. Sunkist is and has been a user of the NRW line. The District is now
in the process of adjusting the rates for users of the NRW line. A resolution is pending which
seeks to adjust the volumetric rates charged to users. The financial effect on Sunkist of the
proposed resolution is approximately $51,233.00. Mr. Catlin is an elected District boardmember
who, absent a conflict of interest, will participate in decisionmaking on this resolution.

The effects of the proposed resolution will apply to all users of the NRW line. The
District provides domestic sewage service to approximately 600,000 citizens, but there are only
55 users of the NRW line within the region. Although Sunkist does do business within the
jurisdiction of the District, it does not do business within that segment of the District which Mr.
Catlin has been elected to represent.

ANALYSIS

1. The Standard for Determining Materiality.

The Political Reform Act was adopted by California voters through the initiative process
in 1974. Included within the Act are conflict-of-interest provisions intended to insure that public
officials, whether elected or appointed, would perform their duties in an impartial manner, free
from any bias attributable to personal financial interests, or to the financial interests of persons
who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) To further this purpose, Section 87100 provides:

File No. A-97-574
Page 3

"No public official, at any level of state or local government shall
make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his
official position to influence a governmental decision in which he
knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest."

A "public official" is defined by the Act to include every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local governmental agency. (Section 82048.) Elected District
boardmembers are public officials within the meaning of the Act. A vote on rate adjustments is
participation in a governmental decision within the meaning of the Act. (Regulation 18700(b).)

An official has a "financial interest" in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on the official or a member of his or her immediate family, or on one of five economic
interests specified in the Act. (Section 87103.) One of these interests is defined at Section
87103(d) as a business entity in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.² As an employee, Mr. Catlin has a "financial
interest" in Sunkist, and we proceed next to decide whether the proposed resolution would have a
foreseeable, material financial effect on Sunkist.

An effect of a decision is "reasonably foreseeable" if there is a substantial likelihood that
it will occur. Certainty is not required, but the effect must be more than a mere possibility.
(Downey Cares v. Downey Community Development Comm. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983, 989;
Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817, 822. Under this standard, it is reasonably foreseeable
that a decision to adjust the rates charged to users of the NRW line will have a financial effect on
those users, and indeed you have told us that the financial effect on Sunkist of this proposed
resolution would be approximately $51,000.

Although some financial effect is reasonably foreseeable in this case, it remains to be
decided whether that effect is also "material." Regulation 18702.1(a) provides that any financial
effect is per se material if the source of income or business entity that constitutes the financial
interest is directly involved in the decision. Subdivision (b) of Regulation 18702.1 provides that:

"A person or business entity is directly involved in a decision
before an official's agency when that person or entity, either
personally or by an agent:

² Regulation 18702.1(a)(4) describes as material any financial effect of $250 or more on the official or the
official's immediate family. This regulation is not applied when the effect on the official is secondary to an impact
on an investment interest in a business entity, but amounts to an alternative standard when the effect does not fall on
a business entity. In the following analysis, we presume that effects fall directly upon the officials' investment
interests. To the extent that this is incorrect, you must consider direct personal effects on the officials and the
officials' immediate families.

File No. A-97-574
Page 4

(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency.
(3) A person or business entity is the subject of a proceeding if a
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with the subject person or business entity."

The decision to adjust these rates is a decision of general application, albeit one that
foreseeably might have a financial effect on any one of the users. In similar cases involving
decisions of general application, we have found that no particular person or property can be
separated out from the class of all potentially affected properties and described as "the subject of
the proceeding." (Irendacosta Advice Letter, No. A-95-371; Gelb Advice Letter, No. A-91-
523.)

The result is that Sunkist's involvement in the rate adjustment decision is indirect, and
the potential materiality of any effect is measured against the criteria given in Regulation
18702.2. (Copy enclosed.) Materiality thresholds are established by this regulation in rough
proportion to the size of the business entity involved. You inform us that Sunkist stock is not
traded on any national exchange, but that its gross revenues for 1996 were $1,025,000,000. The
appropriate materiality benchmarks would then be those given in either subdivision (e), (f), or the
catch-all subdivision (g), which provide as follows:

"(e) For any business entity not covered by subdivisions (a) or (b)
which meets the financial standards for listing on the New York
Stock Exchange, the tests in subdivision (b) may be applied. The
standards are as follows: The business entity has net tangible assets
of at least $18,000,000 and had pre-tax income for the last fiscal
year of at least $2,500,000.
(f) For any business entity not covered by subdivisions (a) or (b)
which meets the financial standards for listing on the National
Association of Securities Dealers National Market List, the tests in
subdivision (c) may be applied. The standards are as follows: The
business entity has net tangible assets of at least $4,000,000 and
had pre-tax income for the last fiscal year of at least $750,000, with
net income from that period of at least $400,000.
(g) For any business entity not covered by subdivisions (a), (b), (c),
(d), (e), or (f):
(1) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the gross
revenues for a fiscal year of $10,000 or more; or
(2) The decision will result in the business entity incurring or
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or

File No. A-97-574
Page 5

(3) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value
of assets or liabilities of $10,000 or more."

Each of these subdivisions operates in the same way. Given its revenues, Sunkist meets
the criteria of subdivision (e) if the value of its net tangible assets is at least $18,000,000. The
materiality tests of subdivision (b) would then apply. The tests for subdivision (b) are:

"(1) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the gross
revenue for a fiscal year of $150,000 or more; or
(2) The decision will result in the business entity incurring or
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating expenses
for a fiscal year in the amount of $50,000 or more; or
(3) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value
of assets or liabilities of $150,000 or more."

Although you have estimated the "financial effect" of the decision on Sunkist to be
$51,233, we do not know whether this sum should be allocated to changes in annual expenses, or
to changes in gross revenue or the value of assets. If allocated entirely to the increase or decrease
of expenses over a fiscal year, the effect would be material; otherwise, it appears that application
of this test would result in a conclusion that the financial effect is not material. You will have to
learn in more detail what is measured in Sunkist's estimation of "financial effect."

If the net tangible assets of Sunkist are valued at less than $18,000,000 but more than
$4,000,000, materiality to Sunkist would be measured under subdivision (f), which applies tests
with lower thresholds; a $30,000 change in gross revenues or liabilities, or the
incurring/avoidance of $7,500 in expenses. If Sunkist does not have assets permitting
consideration under either subdivisions (e) or (f), materiality will be decided under subdivision
(g) in the same manner, but with still lower thresholds.

The determination of materiality under this regulation is straightforward and fact specific.
Given what you know about its revenues, once you learn value of Sunkist's assets you will know
whether to apply the standards given at subdivision (e), (f), or (g).

2. The Public Generally Exception.

Public officials with financial interests materially affected by a decision may still
participate in that decision if the effect on their interests is not distinguishable from the effect on
the public generally. This exception is applicable in a number of factual circumstances described
in Regulations 18703 through 18703.3. The circumstances you present are most appropriately
governed by Regulation 18703(b)(3), which provides as follows:

File No. A-97-574
Page 6

"(b) Special Rule for Rates, Assessments, and Similar Decisions:
The financial effect of a governmental decision on the official's
economic interest is indistinguishable from the decision's effect on
the public generally if any of the following apply:
* * *
(3) The decision is made by the governing board of a water,
irrigation or similar district to establish or adjust assessments,
taxes, fees, charges or rates or other similar decisions, such as the
allocation of services, which are applied on a proportional or
"across-the-board" basis on the official's economic interests and a
significant segment of the property owners or other persons
receiving services from the official's agency using the thresholds
set forth in subdivision (a)(1) above."

The "public" described in this regulation is "property owners or other persons receiving
services" from the District. To determine what constitutes a "significant segment" of that public,
we are referred to the "threshold" standard of Regulation 18703(a)(1)(B). As appropriate to
effects on business entities, the "significant segment" is quantified as:

"[F]ifty percent of all businesses in the jurisdiction or the district
the official represents, so long as the segment is composed of
persons other than a single industry, trade, or profession."

You have recognized the application of this regulation, and state that the approximately
55 users of the NRW line do not amount to 50 percent of the businesses within the District.
However, you point out that the decision affects all users of the line equally and, at least by
implication, you ask whether this fact might not suffice to establish a "significant segment"
appropriate to the matter at hand. We cannot agree with an interpretation that overlooks the
explicit reference in the regulation to the larger public that the official represents. If "the public
generally" were reduced to numerous, separate "user groups" within a larger jurisdiction,
officials with financial interests in particular groups would be able to use their influence to favor
those groups at the expense of others in which they happened to have no financial interest. The
exception would come to swallow the rule. We must therefore conclude that, if the NRW line
does not serve 50 percent or more of businesses within the District, or 50 percent of businesses in
the jurisdiction the official represents, its users do not amount to a significant segment of the
public generally.

If the public generally exception is not otherwise available, you ask whether Mr. Catlin's
circumstances might be treated under Regulation 18703(a)(1)(D), which makes provision for
extending the exception due to "exceptional circumstances regarding the decision." Again, the
answer is no. This provision is a very narrow exception that was adopted in conjunction with
subdivision (c) — applicable to states of emergency — to apply in exceptional circumstances

File No. A-97-574
Page 7

where large numbers of persons were affected. (Bailey Advice Letter No. A-95-230.) The facts
you report do not reflect such exceptional circumstances regarding the decision.

Industries, trades or professions may, under certain circumstances, be considered
significant segments of a population, if they can be classified as the "predominant industry, trade,
or profession in the official's jurisdiction," as referenced at Regulation 18703.2(b). You have
told us that Sunkist is a large operation, employing 403 persons within the District's jurisdiction,
and that it is the seventh largest holder of capacity units in the NRW line. You ask whether
Sunkist can thus be considered a "predominant industry" within the meaning of Regulation
18703.2(b). You have not given us any information on other industries in the jurisdiction.

When a governmental decision will affect an entire industry in substantially the same
manner as it will affect an official's economic interest, the industry is considered to constitute a
significant segment if that industry is a "predominant industry" in the jurisdiction or district.
(Regulation 18703.2.) Regulation 18703.2 does not set any specific criteria for determining
when an industry, trade, or profession is predominant in a given jurisdiction. We rely on the
settled interpretation that the "predominant industry" variation of the public generally exception
is to be construed narrowly. (Woods Advice Letter, No. A-94-164.) Originally, the term
"predominant" was meant to apply to a situation where a local economy was based on one
industry, so that almost any public official would have an economic tie to that industry, trade, or
profession. (Ibid.) In Woods, supra, we advised that real estate sales, while the third most
common type of business in the jurisdiction, was not the basis of the local economy, and
therefore the "predominant industry" variation on the public generally exception did not apply.

The facts which you have provided do not suggest that Sunkist can be considered a
"predominant industry" in the District's jurisdiction, as that term is used in construing the Act.

If you have any other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

Stock Exchange, the tests in subdivision (b) may be applied. The
standards are as follows: The business entity has net tangible assets
of at least $18,000,000 and had pre-tax income for the last fiscal

which meets the financial standards for listing on the National

business entity has net tangible assets of at least $4,000,000 and
had pre-tax income for the last fiscal year of at least $750,000, with
net income from that period of at least $400,000.

governmental decision would be “material” to Sunkist Growers, Inc., a non-stock membership
cooperative of citrus growers?

decisions on rates and charges affect all users of certain District assets? (916) 322-5660

CONCLUSION: 1. Materiality of effect on Sunkist should be measured against the standards given at
